chapter_summary: Sūta resumes the tale: at this moment the two sisters behold Uccaiḥśravas, the supreme jewel among horses, approaching nearby. Uccaiḥśravas was worshipped by all the gods, born from the churning of the nectar (amṛta) — a horse of flood-like strength, swiftest, glorious, ageless, divine, and marked with every auspicious sign. Śaunaka interrupts to ask how the gods churned the amṛta and where this mighty splendid horse-king was born. Sūta answers by describing Mount Meru: a blazing, immovable mass of radiance whose golden peaks throw back the sun's light, ornamented in gold, frequented by gods and gandharvas, immeasurable and unassailable by the unrighteous, roamed by terrible serpents, lit by divine herbs, covering heaven with its height, full of rivers, trees, and singing birds. Upon its jewel-strewn summit the mighty gods ascended and, seated, began to deliberate together for the sake of the nectar, established in austerity and restraint. There Nārāyaṇa spoke to Brahmā as the gods pondered, proposing that the ocean be churned by gods together with the hosts of asuras, declaring that amṛta would arise from the churning of the great ocean. He instructed them to gather all herbs and all jewels and churn the ocean, after which they would obtain the nectar.

story.title: Mount Meru and the Resolve to Churn the Ocean
story.arc: Prompted by Śaunaka's question about the origin of the nectar-born horse Uccaiḥśravas, Sūta recounts how the gods gathered atop the blazing golden summit of Mount Meru. Seated in austerity, they deliberated on how to obtain amṛta. Nārāyaṇa proposes to Brahmā the great plan: gods and asuras together must churn the ocean, gathering all herbs and jewels, so that the nectar will arise. The chapter sets the stage for the great churning of the ocean (samudra manthana).
story.timeline_note: A flashback within the frame narrative: Śaunaka's question sends Sūta back to the primordial churning of the ocean, explaining the origin of Uccaiḥśravas seen by the two sisters.
story.comic_potential: The image of the entire host of gods clambering up a mountain whose peaks are so bright they bat the sun's own rays back at it — then sitting down to hold a committee meeting about how to get immortality — has a gently absurd grandeur.

teachings.teaching: The supreme nectar of immortality is not won by gods alone but requires the joint churning effort of opposed forces — devas and asuras together — gathering all herbs and all jewels before amṛta can arise.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework the churning of the ocean is read as the inner sādhana: the deva and asura forces are the upward and downward currents within, and only by their disciplined, sustained churning (the steady friction of practice) does the nectar of immortal consciousness (amṛta) rise from the depths of one's own being.

teachings.teaching: The deliberation for amṛta is undertaken by the gods 'established in austerity and self-control' (taponiyamasaṁsthitāḥ) — the pursuit of immortality begins from a seat of tapas and restraint, not from craving.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that the rising of the inner nectar is conditioned on niyama and tapas: the seeker first becomes 'seated' and still (the ascent of Meru = the rising of awareness along the inner axis) before the churning bears fruit.

teachings.teaching: Mount Meru, the golden axis blazing at the centre, is unassailable by the unrighteous and unreachable even by the mind of others — the highest ground of realization is barred to the adharmic and to mere mental approach.
teachings.lens_note: Meru is the meru-daṇḍa, the inner spinal axis, in Sharma's reading: a luminous golden column that the lower, scattered, adharma-bound mind cannot scale; only the gathered, austerity-seated awareness ascends to deliberate there.
